metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Re-scope or re-title `contained-dendritic-angora` to make clear its deliverable is a CI step that **extracts and runs the README quickstart fenced block verbatim** (not merely wires probe-rebar.sh), and clarify whether probe-rebar.sh is repurposed as that extractor or replaced by a new script.
findings.finding: Child `contained-dendritic-angora` is titled and described as 'wire the existing golden-path probe into CI' (i.e., running probe-rebar.sh), but the epic's AC and the Drift-proofing mechanism section explicitly require a DIFFERENT artifact: a gate that extracts fenced command blocks from the README quickstart and runs those exact lines. The plan itself explains why these are not equivalent — probe-rebar.sh uses its own internally-correct syntax and does NOT catch wrong text printed in the README. As scoped, the child would deliver a CI gate that passes even when the README contains the broken `session-log` command, contradicting the epic's stated G6/F4 mechanism.
findings.location: Child ticket `contained-dendritic-angora` title/description vs. AC block 'The golden-path gate … is scoped to extract the fenced command block(s) from the README quickstart and execute those exact lines' and 'Reuse note: scripts/probe-rebar.sh already implements the full install→…→close golden path; it just is not wired into CI'
findings.suggested_fix: Update the child title to 'P0: Add CI gate that extracts and executes README quickstart fenced block verbatim' and in its description distinguish the two sub-tasks: (1) author/adapt the extraction script, and (2) wire it into CI — making clear that probe-rebar.sh may be a reference implementation but is not the gate itself.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add CLAUDE.md to 235a's file scope: either update it to link to docs/mcp-reference.md in place of the inline catalog, or explicitly list it as reviewed-and-intentionally-unchanged with a rationale.
findings.finding: Child 235a identifies 'the hand-maintained catalog in CLAUDE.md' as the drift problem but does not include CLAUDE.md in its Files scope for update or deprecation; after 235a lands, the stale MCP catalog in CLAUDE.md would remain, leaving an authoritative-looking but potentially out-of-date tool list for LLM contributors.
findings.location: child 235a-e6c1-e034-4a75 — CLAUDE.md residual reference
findings.suggested_fix: In 235a, add CLAUDE.md to the Files list and include an AC: 'CLAUDE.md's inline MCP tool catalog is replaced with a pointer to docs/mcp-reference.md (or removed).' Alternatively, if CLAUDE.md is intentionally kept as a human-curated supplement, add a note in the ticket that its divergence from the generated reference is acceptable and document why.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Promote dead-link checking from optional to required in f088's AC list, or add a separate child ticket to cover dead-link CI enforcement, so the parent AC is fully satisfied.
findings.finding: The parent acceptance criterion 'Docs-index drift and dead links fail the build' requires dead links to be a hard CI failure, but child f088 marks dead-link checking as only optional ('Optionally also flag obviously-dead relative markdown links'), and no other sibling covers dead-link enforcement; this is a scope-narrowing pattern leaving dead-link rot unguarded.
findings.location: child f088-ef68-9518-4833 — dead-link checking scope vs parent AC
findings.suggested_fix: In f088, add a required AC: 'The check script also validates that relative markdown links in docs/*.md resolve to existing files, and exits non-zero on broken links.' Remove the 'Optionally' qualifier from the What section for this item.

findings.finding: Sibling tickets touch the same file(s) with no ordering edge.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a depends_on/blocks edge to serialize, or partition the file ownership.

findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Revise 0435's 'What' section to clearly state that a README-fenced-block extraction step is required (not mere probe reuse), and update the Files list to include the extraction script or inline CI logic.
findings.finding: Child 0435's 'What' section promotes reusing probe-rebar.sh directly ('Reuse, do not re-author: scripts/probe-rebar.sh … This ticket wires it … into CI'), but AC#4 requires a mechanically different implementation: parsing the README quickstart fenced block and executing those exact extracted lines. The probe uses its own internally-correct syntax, not the README's text — wiring the probe as-is satisfies the first three ACs but explicitly fails the fourth.
findings.location: child 0435-8dce-4e9f-4121 — internal conflict between What section and AC#4
findings.suggested_fix: Clarify 0435's scope: probe-rebar.sh may be used as a reference or execution engine, but the CI job MUST contain a step that parses the README's fenced block and feeds those exact lines to the execution environment. Add a new file (e.g. scripts/run_readme_quickstart.sh or an inline run: block) that performs the extraction and execution, and list it in the ticket's Files section.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the extraction rule for the README fenced-block gate in ticket 0435: name the marker (e.g., an HTML comment sentinel `<!-- quickstart-ci-start -->` / `<!-- quickstart-ci-end -->`, or 'the first ```bash block under `## Quickstart`'), state that the entire block is executed as a shell script (not line-by-line), and confirm multi-line variable capture is in scope.
findings.finding: The fenced-block extraction mechanism — the plan's core drift-proofing mechanism — has no specified extraction rule: the README Quickstart section contains three fenced blocks (```bash, ```python, ```json), and the plan does not state which block(s) to extract, what delimiter or structural marker identifies 'the quickstart bash block' vs. any other bash block in the README, or how the extractor handles multi-line bash with shell variable interpolation (required once b55f repairs the quickstart to capture the created ticket id). Without this specification, ticket 0435 implementers will make ad-hoc choices that may be brittle or incorrect.
findings.location: AC: 'The golden-path CI gate runs the literal commands extracted from the README quickstart fenced block' / Drift-proofing mechanism subsection / child ticket contained-dendritic-angora (0435)
findings.suggested_fix: Add an HTML comment sentinel pair around the bash quickstart block (e.g. `<!-- quickstart:bash-start -->` … `<!-- quickstart:bash-end -->`) as the extraction contract; the CI gate extracts between those markers and runs the result as `bash -euo pipefail`. Document this sentinel in the AC for 0435 and in a brief README contributor note so future editors know the sentinel must be preserved.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify in the plan whether 'dead links' means (a) docs not listed in the index or (b) hyperlinks with broken targets (404s), and if (b) add a validated finding or explicitly call it a proactive gate beyond the audit scope.
findings.finding: The AC line 'Dead links fail the build' introduces scope beyond what the Context P2 finding describes. P2 is scoped to 'incomplete index (4 unlinked living docs) and mixes living references with historical evidence' — no mention of dead/broken hyperlinks. The AC's dead-link requirement has no backing validated finding in Context and no described remediation approach.
findings.location: Context § Validated findings (P2); AC line: 'Docs-index drift and dead links fail the build (docs-index CI gate)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Remove or consolidate the near-duplicate 'every validated defect corrected' statement — either keep it only in AC (the verifiable checkpoint) or make the Success Criteria version clearly higher-level (e.g. reframe as the goal, not a checklist item).
findings.finding: 'Every validated defect is corrected' appears twice — once in Success Criteria ('Every validated defect above is corrected against origin/main') and once in AC ('Every validated defect (P0/P1/P2 above) is corrected on origin/main') — as near-duplicate requirements across two sections, both acting as verification checkpoints for the same outcome.
findings.location: Success Criteria bullet 1; AC line 5
findings.suggested_fix: Keep the statement only in the AC (where it is verifiable and actionable) and replace the Success Criteria bullet with a goal-oriented framing such as 'All P0/P1/P2 audit findings have documented corrections landed on origin/main.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly decide and document the fate of `scripts/probe-rebar.sh` after the golden-path CI gate lands: either (a) delete it, (b) convert it to call the same extracted-commands mechanism, or (c) keep it as a local-dev convenience and add a comment noting it is not the CI gate.
findings.finding: The plan announces that the new golden-path CI gate will 'extract the fenced command block(s) from the README quickstart and execute those exact lines' rather than wire in the existing probe-rebar.sh. This leaves probe-rebar.sh's fate unspecified — it may become a stranded, stale script that describes the same golden path with its own internally-correct syntax but is never executed in CI and may diverge from the README over time.
findings.location: Context — 'Reuse note' section; AC — 'Drift-proofing mechanism' section

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Declare explicit depends_on relationships among the five test.yml-editing children (0435, e205, e866, 235a, f088) to enforce the serial stack order claimed by the epic.
findings.finding: Children editing .github/workflows/test.yml (0435, e205, e866, 235a, f088) do not declare mutual dependencies on each other; the serial ordering is asserted only at the epic level, leaving individual children schedulable concurrently and risking merge conflicts or misordered CI job wiring.
findings.location: children 0435, e205, e866, 235a, f088 — all touch .github/workflows/test.yml
findings.suggested_fix: Add depends_on: [e205] to e866 (or similar chain), and continue the chain through 235a → f088 → 0435, matching the epic's asserted serial order. At minimum, document the required merge order in each child ticket.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add depends_on: [9d81] to child 6755 so the intent router is built on top of the completed, de-mixed index.
findings.finding: Child 6755 edits docs/README.md but does not declare depends_on 9d81, even though both tickets make structural edits to the same file; concurrent execution risks a merge conflict and could produce an index where 6755's router sits atop an incomplete or un-de-mixed index.
findings.location: child 6755-689b-72c2-4dac — docs/README.md ordering vs 9d81
findings.suggested_fix: In child 6755's ticket metadata, add depends_on: 9d81-2afd-5b67-4bf2 to enforce that 9d81 lands first.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define 'live registries' for CLI and MCP in the AC (e.g., 'CLI registry = installed console_scripts entry-points; MCP registry = server tool-list introspection output') so the drift-gate success condition is unambiguously evaluable.
findings.finding: The AC 'Generated CLI + MCP references drift-gate against their live registries' uses the term 'live registries' without defining what those registries are (e.g., Python entry-points metadata, MCP server introspection output, a static manifest file), leaving an executor unable to determine whether the criterion is satisfied without stopping to investigate.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — final bullet: 'Generated CLI + MCP references drift-gate against their live registries'
findings.suggested_fix: Expand the AC bullet to: 'Generated CLI references drift-gate against the installed console_scripts entry-points (or equivalent manifest); generated MCP references drift-gate against the MCP server's introspected tool list — both gates run in CI and fail the build on mismatch.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the concrete artifact that satisfies 'captured as an idea' (e.g., 'a GitHub issue labelled `idea` in this repo') so the AC is unambiguously evaluable.
findings.finding: The AC 'Lower-value inventory work (Python-API / config-key) is captured as an `idea`, not shipped speculatively' does not define what artifact type 'idea' maps to in this project's tracking system, making it ambiguous whether a GitHub issue with a specific label, a separate markdown file, or some other artifact satisfies the criterion.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — bullet: 'Lower-value inventory work (Python-API / config-key) is captured as an `idea`, not shipped speculatively'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'captured as an `idea`' with 'filed as a GitHub issue with the `idea` label (or equivalent tracker entry) and NOT included in this epic's milestone'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a plan-body section (or ticket reference) describing the docs-index CI gate: which file is the authoritative index, what tool/script checks completeness and dead links, and which ticket owns this gate — mirroring the detail given for the golden-path gate.
findings.finding: The docs-index CI gate is named as a deliverable in both Success Criteria ('New CI gates make... docs-index drift fail the build automatically') and AC ('Docs-index drift and dead links fail the build') but the plan body contains no implementation description, no approach, and no ticket reference for it — unlike the golden-path gate (fully described + ticket 0435) and generated-reference gates (tickets e866, 235a). This is an orphan AC criterion with no covering described work.
findings.location: Success Criteria bullet 2; AC line: 'Docs-index drift and dead links fail the build (docs-index CI gate)'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'Docs-index drift-proofing mechanism' paragraph parallel to the existing 'Drift-proofing mechanism' paragraph: name the authoritative index file (e.g. docs/README.md), specify the check (e.g. a script that enumerates *.md under docs/ and verifies each appears in the index, plus a link-checker like markdown-link-check), reference the owning ticket ID, and name the CI job step.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a planned deliverable (doc section, ADR, or CONTRIBUTING note) that describes how the drift gates work, what triggers them, and how a contributor updates them when intentionally changing a gated surface.
findings.finding: The new drift-gate pattern — README fenced-block extraction, docs-index gate, and generated CLI+MCP reference gates — is a new architectural CI mechanism that contributors must understand to maintain (e.g., when adding a new CLI command or changing the quickstart). No ADR or contributor-facing doc explaining how these gates work, how to update them, and how to add new generated references is planned.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — 'Drift-proofing mechanism' section and 'Generated CLI + MCP references drift-gate' AC line
findings.suggested_fix: Add a brief 'Maintaining the drift gates' section to CONTRIBUTING.md (or an inline README note) as a deliverable in the final child ticket (e.g., b5a4 or f088), covering: (1) how the fenced-block extractor identifies the quickstart block, (2) how to regenerate CLI/MCP reference docs, (3) how to update the docs-index manifest.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] For the leaf.py bash-engine removal, add an explicit confirmation that the code path is provably dead (e.g., grep/coverage evidence) and state the rollback path (e.g., 'revert the commit; no data migration needed') in the child ticket scope.
findings.finding: The plan describes removing 'stale bash-engine language in CI comments + leaf.py' as a P1 fix, which is a code change (not purely a doc change). If any code path in leaf.py is live rather than truly dead, this constitutes an unacknowledged behavioral change with no stated rollback path.
findings.location: Context — 'Validated findings' P1 bullet; implied child ticket scope
findings.suggested_fix: In the child ticket for the leaf.py edit, add a pre-condition check: run `grep -r 'bash' . --include='*.py'` and confirm zero live call-sites, then note 'rollback: git revert <sha>; no schema/data change'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Document the README↔CI-gate coupling: specify the sentinel/marker convention (e.g., an HTML comment tag like `<!-- quickstart-commands-start -->`) that the extractor uses to locate the exact fenced block, and note in both the plan and a code comment that README authors must preserve this marker — making the contract explicit and mitigating silent breakage.
findings.finding: The new golden-path CI gate introduces an unacknowledged tight coupling between the README's markdown fence structure (which block to extract, how the quickstart section is delimited) and the CI extraction logic. If someone restructures the README quickstart (splits blocks, adds prose, changes section heading), the extractor silently breaks or picks the wrong block — but the plan contains no mitigation, no sentinel/marker convention, and no note that README authors must preserve the extractable structure.
findings.location: AC section — 'The golden-path CI gate runs the literal commands extracted from the README quickstart fenced block' and 'Drift-proofing mechanism' subsection
findings.suggested_fix: Add a clearly named HTML comment pair (e.g., `<!-- ci-quickstart-start -->` / `<!-- ci-quickstart-end -->`) around the exact fenced block in the README quickstart. The CI extractor targets that marker rather than heuristically finding 'the first/only fence.' Document this contract in the plan (and ideally in a brief comment in the CI script) so future README editors know the marker is load-bearing.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC that defines the minimum observable form of the audience router (e.g., 'docs/README.md contains a table or section with one entry point per named audience — human client, LLM client, human contributor, LLM contributor — verified by reviewer checklist or a grep in CI') so the intent-based routing goal has an end-state-observable proof.
findings.finding: The 'intent-based router for the four audiences' is promised in Success Criteria as a deliverable but has no corresponding AC that verifies the router is correctly implemented or useful — only that the index is complete and drift-gated (dead links fail CI). The quality of the routing content itself is unverifiable from the ACs.
findings.location: Success Criteria (paragraph 3): 'The docs index is complete and clearly separates living reference from historical evidence, with an intent-based router for the four audiences.' vs Acceptance Criteria: 'Docs-index drift and dead links fail the build (docs-index CI gate).'
findings.suggested_fix: Add an AC such as: '[ ] `docs/README.md` contains an audience-routing section with at least one explicit entry point for each of the four named audiences (human clients, LLM clients, human contributors, LLM contributors); a `grep`-based CI check or PR reviewer checklist item confirms all four are present.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add one sentence of positive rationale to the 'Drift-proofing mechanism' section explaining why extract-and-exec is preferred over static subcommand validation (e.g., it also catches argument-value errors, alias resolution, and init/create/claim/close sequencing — not just subcommand name correctness).
findings.finding: The plan does not state a positive rationale for the extract-and-exec approach over the structurally different alternative of statically validating README fenced commands against the live CLI's `--help` or `--version` output. The plan argues well against probe-rebar.sh alone (doesn't catch README text), but gives no positive argument for why live execution (requires clean install, throwaway git repo, live engine, hermetic env) is preferred over a lightweight static comparison (parse fenced block, check each command name against `rebar --help`). The absence of a positive rationale leaves the approach choice undefended.
findings.location: Drift-proofing mechanism subsection / Success Criteria: 'New CI gates make quickstart-command rot … fail the build automatically'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Evaluate established README-execution tools (e.g., `runme`, `pytest-markdown-docs`, `cram`, `mdsh`) against the gate's specific requirements (throwaway git repo, installed-rebar, REBAR_ROOT injection, skip non-bash blocks); either adopt one or document concisely why a custom extractor is necessary.
findings.finding: The golden-path CI gate (ticket 0435) introduces a custom README fenced-block parser-and-runner as a novel CI pattern, but the plan neither considers nor dismisses well-established prior art that does exactly this: `runme` (runme.dev), `cram`, `mdsh`, and `pytest-markdown-docs` all execute fenced code blocks from markdown documents in CI. The plan asserts the custom approach without justifying why an off-the-shelf tool is insufficient.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria / 'The golden-path CI gate runs the literal commands extracted from the README quickstart fenced block'; ticket contained-dendritic-angora/0435 AC item 4; Drift-proofing mechanism section

findings.finding: The plan silently drops the 'symptom->fix troubleshooting table' half of ticket 6755's scope. The session log names the ticket as 'Add intent-based router + symptom->fix troubleshooting table to docs index', expressing two distinct deliverables. The plan's Success Criteria and Acceptance Criteria reference only the 'intent-based router for the four audiences' and never mention the troubleshooting table.

The plan's validated-findings section lists the dependency-count correction as targeting 'README and docs/config.md', silently omitting pyproject.toml, which is explicitly named in the session log ticket 319a title ('across docs + pyproject').

The plan's core AC — 'the golden-path CI gate runs the literal commands extracted from the README quickstart fenced block' — asserts a novel parse-and-exec mechanism that does not yet exist in the repo (no script in scripts/ implements README fenced-block extraction), does not specify which of the three fenced blocks (bash, python, json) are executed, and does not specify the extraction tool or approach. Additionally, the bash fenced block currently contains `rebar claim reel-lot-tea` — a hard-coded alias that will not exist in a throwaway fresh repo — meaning the gate would fail for the wrong reason if wired before the quickstart is repaired. The child ticket for this gate (0435/contained-dendritic-angora) has an empty deps list, so no hard sequencing guard prevents the gate from being wired before b55f lands.
findings.location: AC section: 'The golden-path CI gate runs the literal commands extracted from the README quickstart fenced block' / Drift-proofing mechanism section / child ticket contained-dendritic-angora (0435) deps=[]
findings.suggested_fix: Add a new script (e.g. `scripts/run_readme_quickstart.sh`) that: (1) extracts only the first `bash` fenced block from `## Quickstart` in README.md using `awk`/`sed` or a Python helper, (2) runs it in a fresh `mktemp -d` repo, and (3) is invoked from the 0435 CI job. Add `depends_on: [b55f]` to ticket 0435 so CI wiring can only land after the quickstart is repaired to use a dynamically-created ticket id rather than the hard-coded alias.

findings.finding: Children 9d81 and 6755 each have an AC requiring they 'pass the docs-index CI gate (sibling ticket f088)' but neither declares a dependency on f088. If f088 is not yet implemented when 9d81 or 6755 are reviewed, their AC cannot be verified — and if f088 is merged after them, an intermediate state could exist where the gate does not yet enforce the index completeness that 9d81/6755 claim to deliver.
findings.location: child 9d81-2afd-5b67-4bf2, child 6755-689b-72c2-4dac
findings.suggested_fix: Make f088 a prerequisite for closing 9d81 and 6755 by adding depends_on f088 to each, consistent with how b5a4 explicitly depends_on e866 and 235a.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
